coredata.dc:title: Design of chalcopyrite-type CuFeSe2 nanocrystals: Microstructure, magnetism, photoluminescence and sensing performances
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Nengtao
Li, Ye
Zeng, Min
Gao, Jinwei
Tang, Yiping
Zeng, Zhi
Zheng, Yuhui
coredata.dc:description: Most of reported I-III-VI2 type chalcopyrites have been developed as indispensible materials for potential application in photovoltaic solar cells, magnetism or thermoeletrics. The exploration of its photoluminescence potentials has been very limited and the chemical sensing using fluorescence to signal a recognition event is realized by way of CuFeSe2 nanocrystals. A novel solution-based synthesis strategy has been developed for the preparation of CuFeSe2 quantum dots (QDs) via oleylamine and dodecanethiol as precursors. QDs possess an intense blue luminescence at 431 nm and excitation-dependent features are recorded. The nanocrystals exhibit transformation from ferromagnetism to paramagnetism state between 4 K and 298 K. Another distinct advantage of the quantum dots will be the signaling pathway in response to external analytes. It gives rise to a rapid and selective assay of Cr2O7 2- through an “on-off” switching process. A linear equation can be obtained in the range from 0 to 47.5 μM and the detection limit has been determined to be 0.46 μM. This cost-effective method will pave the way for the efficient synthesis of ternary chalcopyrites and provide suitable chemical routes for sensing purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuFeSe2
Nanocrystals
Magnetic
Optical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity of the chemosensor for Cr2O7 2- detection
Selectivity of the chemosensor for Cr2O7 2- detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CuFeSe2; the blue vertical is the standard literature data.
(Left) HR-TEM images of CuFeSe2 QDs and (Right) DLS curve of CuFeSe2 QDs.
Hysteresis loops of CuFeSe2 QDs taken at 4 K and 298 K.
UV–visible absorption spectra of CuFeSe2 QDs (red); Excitation (green) and emission (blue) spectra of CuFeSe2 QDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CuFeSe2 QDs in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of Cr2O7 2- ions (0–47.5 μM). (b) The plot of fluorescence intensity change at 431 nm upon the addition with Cr2O7 2- ions. (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the CuFeSe2 QDs in the presence and absence of Cr2O7 2- ions, respectively).
Graph of the effect of different metal ions on the PL intensity of the CuFeSe2 QDs.
Decay curves in the absence (red) and presence of Cr2O7 2- ions (green).
TEM images of (a) CuFeSe2 QDs dispersed in ethanol: water = 2:1 and CuFeSe2 QDs with addition of (b) 5 μM Cr2O7 2- ions.
Schematic illustration of preparation for CuFeSe2 QDs system and sensing processes.
Determination data of Cr2O7 2- ions in tap water.

head.ce:title: Design of chalcopyrite-type CuFeSe2 nanocrystals: Microstructure, magnetism, photoluminescence and sensing performances

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most of reported I-III-VI2 type chalcopyrites have been developed as indispensible materials for potential application in photovoltaic solar cells, magnetism or thermoeletrics. The exploration of its photoluminescence potentials has been very limited and the chemical sensing using fluorescence to signal a recognition event is realized by way of CuFeSe2 nanocrystals. A novel solution-based synthesis strategy has been developed for the preparation of CuFeSe2 quantum dots (QDs) via oleylamine and dodecanethiol as precursors. QDs possess an intense blue luminescence at 431 nm and excitation-dependent features are recorded. The nanocrystals exhibit transformation from ferromagnetism to paramagnetism state between 4 K and 298 K. Another distinct advantage of the quantum dots will be the signaling pathway in response to external analytes. It gives rise to a rapid and selective assay of Cr2O7 2- through an “on-off” switching process. A linear equation can be obtained in the range from 0 to 47.5 μM and the detection limit has been determined to be 0.46 μM. This cost-effective method will pave the way for the efficient synthesis of ternary chalcopyrites and provide suitable chemical routes for sensing purposes.

A novel solution-based synthesis strategy has been developed for the preparation of CuFeSe2 quantum dots (QDs) via oleylamine and dodecanethiol as precursors. QDs possess an intense blue luminescence at 431 nm and excitation-dependent features are recorded. It is observed the magnetic properties and the nanocrystals exhibit transformation from ferromagnetism to paramagnetism between 4 K and 298 K. It gives rise to a rapid and selective assay of Cr2O7 2- through an “on-off” switching process.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: In last decade, metal-semiconductors such as I-III-VI2 ternary chalcopyrites were classified as indispensable and multi-functional materials with unique chemical and physical properties [1–3]. Especially CuInS2, CuInSe2, AgInS2 and CuFeS2 nanostructures received much attention [4–7] and were utilized for photo-catalysis [8], thermoelectric properties [9], nanodevices [10] and biomedicine [11]. Compared with early-reported semiconductor materials (CdS and CsPbCl3), these ternary semiconductors do not contain highly toxic elements and the health risks are largely suppressed [12,13]. In the field of I-III-VI2 materials, the study of copper iron selenium (CuFeSe2) chalcogenides caused considerable interest very recently [14]. As a new alternative, CuFeSe2 possesses a narrow band gap of 0.16 eV [15], which leads to specific optical, electric and magnetic properties. Therefore, some literatures have discussed its application in magnetic photoelectric properties [16] and photothermal therapy [17]. However, the optical activity and fluorescence features of such ternary chalcopyrites were rarely studied. It is valuable to explore its photophysical performance following exposure to ultra-violet irradiation. In this research, ultra-small CuFeSe2 nanoparticles were assembled via a solution-based synthesis strategy in the presence of oleyamine and dodecanethiol (DDT). The general high-temperature solid state method was avoided. At room temperature, upon the excitation at 359 nm, the emission peak of CuFeSe2 quantum dots (QDs) was located at 431 nm. This simple, safe and cost-effective synthetic route was described in Scheme 1.

With the development of industrialization, environmental pollution has generated more and more attention in scientific field. Therefore, it is appealing that we could employ the optical species to actively monitor the concentration of contaminants in practical life. Chromium (Cr) is usually used in diverse manufacturing procedures, such as tanning, electroplating, ore refining, wood preserving and textile dye manufacturing battery production [18–20]. It is well accepted that low concentration of dichromate can be harmful to humankind, including the damage of mucous membrane, skin and so on [21]. In this regard, a sensitive and efficient analytical method is needed to detect Cr (VI) content in the environment and biological fluid. Compared with reported approaches, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [22] and high-performance liquid chromatography [23], fluorescence signal has been considered to be the most powerful transduction way to respond the chemical recognition event.

Herein, novel inorganic CuFeSe2 QDs were tested to detect the content of Cr2O7 2- in aqueous solution. As to sensing techniques, the present activity is mainly concentrated on the design of coordination polymers [24] or synthetic molecules [25]. In comparison to conventional organic frameworks, inorganic nanocrystals possess several distinct merits, such as tunable emission color, strong absorption, appropriate stability and solution processing [26–33]. To the best of our knowledge, no reports have been focused on the detection of dichromate ions with use of CuFeSe2 QDs. Accordingly, the unique property of metal-semiconductors will provide new opportunities to engineer quantum-sized probes for monitoring more analytes in different mediums.

Iron chloridetetrahydrate (FeCl2, 99.7%), cupric chloride dehydrate (CuCl2·H2O, AR), Se powders (Se,99%), oleyamine (C18:80–90%), 1-Dodecanethiol (98%), ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without further purification.

Photoluminescence spectra were measured by using a Hitachi-4600 spectrophotometer with the xenon lamp as the light source (Japan). In all cases, the scan speed was fixed at 1500 nm/min. The excitation and emission were recorded at slit width of 5.0 nm. UV–vis spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a Japan Science Ultima IV X-Ray Powder Diffractometer. TEM images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope. Particle size was also obtained with dynamic light scattering (DLS) using particle size analyzer (Malvern Instrument, UK). The magnetic properties of the CuFeS2 QDs were measured at room temperature using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, BHV-50HTI). Lifetimes were measured with a Transient/Stationary Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS-920, UK). Element mapping images were revealed by a Phenom Pro X Desktop Scanning Electron Microscope (USA).

FeCl2·4H2O (19.88 mg) and CuCl2 (17.05 mg) were added to a two necked flask containing 1.5 mL oleylamine as solvent. The reaction mixture was heated to 100 °C under vacuum for 20 min to remove water. The reaction was heated to 180 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen and kept stirring for about 30 min. Then, 1.5 mL of dodecanethiol (DDT) was added to the reaction mixture and the reaction mixture was heated at 120 °C to initiate the nucleation of CuFeSe2 QDs. After adding the thiol, the solution color turned to bright yellow in five seconds.

Se powder (40 mg) was added to 50 mL three-necked flask containing 1.5 mL oleylamine. This flask was held at 120 °C under nitrogen for 30 min. Then, the yellow solution (copper and iron precursors) in oleylamine was injected dropwise to the Se powder in the oleylamine. After the addition, the solution became black rapidly. Finally, the powder was collected by centrifugation, ultrasonically washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times and dried in vacuum at 70 °C for 24 h.

Se powder (40 mg) and CuCl2 (17.05 mg) were added to a two necked flask containing 1.2 mL oleylamine as solvent. The reaction mixture was heated to 100 °C under vacuum for 20 min to remove water. The reaction mixture was heated to 200 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen and kept stirring for about 30 min. Then, 1.0 mL of dodecanethiol (DDT) was added to the reaction mixture and the reaction mixture was heated at 120 °C to initiate the nucleation of CuFeSe2 QDs. After adding the thiol, color turned to bright yellow in five seconds.

FeCl2·4H2O (19.88 mg) was added to 50 mL three-necked flask containing 1.2 mL oleylamine. This flask was held at 120 °C under nitrogen for 30 min. Then, the Fe-precursor solution in oleylamine was injected dropwise to the mixture solution (copper and Se power) in the oleylamine. After the addition at 200 °C, the solution became black rapidly. Finally, the powder was collected by centrifugation, ultrasonically washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times and dried in vacuum at 70 °C for 24 h.

CuFeSe2 NCs were fabricated via a two-step procedure. The phase composition of the formed CuFeSe2 nanocrystals was obtained by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1, all diffraction peaks were perfectly indexed by the standard data of CuFeSe2 NCs in tetragonal phase (JCPDS No. 81–1959), suggesting that the assembled CuFeSe2 NCs possessed high purity and good crystallinity [17]. In addition, the Cu, Fe and Se elements were observed in as-synthesized CuFeSe2 nanocrystals by EDS and elemental mapping graphs ( Fig. 2), which further supported presence and distribution of these elements in the nanocrystals (Fig. S1). The elemental contents of copper, iron and selenium in the nanodomains were determined to be 25.62%, 24.45% and 49.93% respectively. Therefore, the exact molar ratio of copper/iron/selenium would be calculated as 1.05:1:2.04 from the elemental analysis. The collected results are in agreement to the stoichiometric composition of CuFeSe2 (1:1:2).

With the aim of studying microstructure, morphology and size of the as-prepared CuFeSe2 NCs, the particles were investigated by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The eskebornite sample was dispersed homogenously in aqueous solution and possessed uniform particle shape 1–2 nm in diameter ( Fig. 3a). To our knowledge, it is very difficult to fabricate the tiny nanoparticles with ultra-small dimensions (less than 2 nm) and such TEM results are consistent with the broaden peaks in the XRD pattern. In addition, the zeta potential is used to determine the charge presence on the surface of the nanoparticle in aqueous solution, which may have control their stability. In Fig. S2, the zeta potential (ξ) of CuFeSe2 QDs was around + 13.82 mV based on the presence of -NH2 groups from oleylamine on the surface. Moreover, they can be well-separated and their hydrodynamic size was in the range from 1.3 nm to 2.1 nm. The particle distribution was explored by dynamic light scattering (DLS) in Fig. 3b and the collected curve was coincided with the above results of TEM.

The vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) is generally employed to obtain magnetization of magnetic nanoparticles [34] and it can detect the whole magnetization instead of specific element property [35]. To verify the magnetic response of the CuFeSe2 nanoparticles, the hysteresis loops obtained were measured at 4 and 298 K and between −10k Oe and + 10k Oe, and the results were presented in Fig. 4. The paramagnetic characteristics with a magnetization value of 0.46 emu/g were found at 298 K. The ferromagnetic characteristics with a magnetization value of 1.45 emu/g at 4 K were observed in Fig. 4. According to previous reports [36–38], the magnetic properties of CuFeSe2 indicated that it is paramagnetic from room temperature down to 70–79 K. In the present study, it was found that the magnetic property converted from paramagnetic to ferromagnetic from 298 to 4 K. Such transition from paramagnetism to ferromagnetism indicated that this nanoscale entity will provide an alternative approach to magnetic application such as electromagnetic material and a contrast agent for MRI.

Although considerable interests were concentrated on the optical activity of quantum dots, the information on spectroscopic properties of ternary chalcopyrites (CuFeSe2) is very limited. In this work, absorption and fluorescence spectra of CuFeSe2 QDs were recorded. In ultra-violet spectrum, the nanocrystals exhibited two absorption bands at 260 and 310 nm. Under the excitation at 359 nm, an intense blue emission band at 431 nm was observed. During the excitation-dependent experiments, the emission curves shifted from 400 nm to 480 nm due to the excitation wavelength change from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. S3). This noticeable red shift in emission peaks suggested the presence of nanocrystals with different particle sizes. It may also arise from the distribution of multiple surface defects. We further performed the stability experiments at different pH conditions. It is found that photoluminescence intensity of CuFeSe2 QDs is slightly higher at neutral conditions (pH from 6 to 8) (Fig. S4). But in a wide range of pH values, the as-prepared CuFeSe2 QDs could maintain the stability from pH = 3 to pH = 12. This fact implies that the nanocrystals might be significant for the potential application in biological sensing ( Fig. 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the chemosensor for Cr2O7 2- detection
ce:section.ce:para: An appealing strategy was developed to study its anion recognition behavior. In the presence of Cr2O7 2-, the blue band at 431 nm decreased substantially and an “on-off” change was monitored ( Fig. 6a). To figure out the detection sensitivity of the proposed method, the correlation between the reduction in emission intensity and the concentration of Cr2O7 2- was recorded. A linear relationship between the two parameters was detected, as given in Fig. 6b, and the linear equation was y = 12.62 × +36.5. The corresponding calibration curve in the range of 0–47.5 µM can be observed and the linear relative coefficient was R2 = 0.9939. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.465 µM with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. It confirmed that the CuFeSe2 QDs are specific to the recognition of Cr2O7 2- and the novel nanoplatform suitable for detection of such polluting anions can be established. In order to figure out the size dependence, the larger particles with average diameter of 11 nm were also synthesized (Fig. S5). The sensing ability for Cr2O7 2- was compared and the determined detection limit (3.59 μM) was higher than the small quantum dots (Fig. S6). The results showed the particle control raised significant effects on the recognition event and CuFeSe2 QDs (1–2 nm) was more favorable for the detection of Cr2O7 2-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the chemosensor for Cr2O7 2- detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the nano-probe, various metal ions and anions were used at the same conditions, including Ag+, Ba2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, F-, Br-, Cl-, I-, SO3 2-, AcO-, NO3 -, H2O2, HClO, MnO4 -, CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2- (Fig. S7). The blank sample represented system without metal ions and all the tested anions were maintained to be 50 µM. In Fig. 7, an obvious quenching effect was detected for the presence of Cr2O7 2- and the fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable upon the addition of other ions. These results supported that the interfering chemical species had almost no influence on the emission features. In this way, it might be promising for detecting dichromate in more complicated and harsh environments.

To clarify the fluorescence quenching dynamics of CuFeSe2 QDs upon the dichromate addition in aqueous solution, luminescence decay curves were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 8. The lifetime in the absence or presence of dichromate is determined to be 3.21 ns (χ2 = 0.998) and 3.06 ns (χ2 = 0.982), respectively, by using a monoexponential fit. These two values are very close and it was indicated that the emission of CuFeSe2 QDs would be switched off via a static quenching process [39].

For the sake of further exploring this effect, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed in the absence and presence of Cr2O7 2- ions. As provided in Fig. 9a, the CuFeSe2 QDs were monodispersed in aqueous solution with an average diameter of 1.5–2.0 nm. After the addition of 50 μM Cr2O7 2- ions, the microstructure exhibited drastic change and the granules aggregated to large aggregates (Fig. 9b). Consequently, the aggregated-induced-quenching effect was detected.

The interactions between CuFeSe2 QDs and the analyte were also studied. In the UV–vis absorption line of Cr2O7 2-, the two bands at 362 and 445 nm were observed (Fig. S8, green line). It was clear that the emitted lights of CuFeSe2 QDs could be absorbed by Cr2O7 2- species due to the spectral overlaps and the inner filter effect caused the quenching process [40,41]. We also compared the XRD pattern before and after the addition of Cr2O7 2-, the results exhibited that the diffraction peaks were almost identical and the crystalline phase and microstructure remained unchanged (Fig. S9). Therefore, it is proposed that no oxidation reaction would occur in the presence of dichromate ions.

To further verify the practicability of fluorescence probe, the calibration curve method was used to evaluate the concentration of Cr2O7 2-in tap water. In blank sample, no Cr2O7 2- could be identified. The different Cr2O7 2- levels in the spiked samples were determined based on the standard curve. The results showed that the recovery of Cr2O7 2- was in the range of 99.60–102.1% ( Table 1). The collected results proved the reliability and accuracy of the proposed nanosensor for Cr2O7 2- determination.

Quantum dots are capable of generating the emission and absorption properties due to band-gap effects. In this study, we selected CuFeSe2 QDs as the example to explore the microstructure, crystalline phase, morphology and magnetism. The temperature dependence of magnetization was investigated and the magnetic properties evolved from paramagnetism (4 K) to ferromagnetism (298 K). More importantly, the mono-dispersed nanoparticles exhibited intensive blue luminescence under the excitation at 359 nm. The optical features could be well controlled by varying its excitation wavelengths. Moreover, the presence of Cr2O7 2- enabled the emission change of the nanocrystals and a switched-off effect was observed. This remarkable progress in the application of I-III-VI2 type chalcopyrites for chemical sensing would shed new lights on detection of more targets in the fields of bio-analysis and environmental protection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: J. W. thanks grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC)-Guangdong Joint funding support (No. U1801256) and Innovation team project by the Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009). Q. M. appreciates the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21371063). N.T. thanks for the support of Innovation Project of Graduate School of South China Normal University (2018LKXM027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jssc.2019.01.015
